introduction.title: Vietnamese Modal Verbs
content: Modal verbs express ability ('can'), necessity ('must'), permission ('may'), and desire ('want'). In Vietnamese, they're regular verbs that come BEFORE the main verb — no conjugation, no form changes.
The same word 'được' can mean both 'can' (ability) and 'may' (permission), depending on context. Similarly, 'phải' is both 'must' (necessity) and 'have to' (obligation).
Pattern: Subject + Modal + Verb + (Object). Example: 'Tôi có thể đi' (I can go).

notes: Literally 'have' + 'ability' — the general 'can' for ability and possibility.
Placed before the main verb.
More formal than 'được' for ability.
Can combine with other modals: 'có thể phải' (might have to), 'có thể nên' (might should).
Used specifically for learned skills, not general ability.
Implies you've learned the skill — different from 'có thể' which is broader.
Example: "I can go today: Tôi ~~biết~~ __có thể__ đi hôm nay" (biết is for learned skills, có thể for situational can).
After the verb = ability/success: 'làm __được__' (can do). Before the verb with 'không' = prohibition: '__không được__ làm' (must not do).
Same word as the positive passive marker — context decides.
Position matters: '__được__ làm' (be allowed to do, passive) vs 'làm __được__' (can do it, ability).
Negated 'không được' = 'not allowed / must not' — a prohibition.
As a passive marker, 'được' means 'to receive/benefit from' (see Passive Voice module).
Placed before the verb.
Often used in negative: 'không dám' (don't dare).
Implies courage or audacity — or lack of it.

examples.vietnamese: Tôi có thể nói tiếng Việt
Ở đây không được hút thuốc

notes: The strongest necessity word — like English 'must'.
Placed before the verb: 'phải đi' (must go).
Implies obligation or no choice.
Softer than 'phải' — gives advice, not commands.
Used to suggest the best course of action.
Negative form 'không nên' = 'shouldn't'.
Advice, not obligation: 'Bạn __nên__ đi ngay' (should go — advice) vs 'Bạn phải đi ngay' (must go — no choice).
Expresses need rather than obligation.
Can be followed by a verb or a noun: 'cần tiền' (need money).
Less forceful than 'phải'.

examples.english: I have to go to work

sections.title: Desire & Intention
sections.description: Expressing wishes, preferences, plans, and readiness

notes: The standard 'want' — placed before a verb or noun.
Stronger than 'thích'.
Used for general likes and preferences.
Can take a verb or noun.
Softer and less urgent than 'muốn'.
Expresses a plan or intention, not a firm commitment.
Less certain than 'sẽ' (will) — more like 'thinking of doing'.
Plans, not obligations: 'Tôi __định__ đi' (I plan to go — my choice) vs 'Tôi phải đi' (I must go — no choice).
Literally 'ready' + 'ready' — emphasizes full readiness.
Can express both physical readiness and willingness.
Placed before the verb.

examples.vietnamese: Tôi định đi Hà Nội tuần sau
examples.english: I plan to go to Hanoi next week
examples.literalEnglish: I plan go Hanoi week next
